title: Property Due Diligence Checklist Before You Pay the Token Amount
author: Adv. Kunal Deshmukh
content: A token amount feels small, but it often becomes the point where negotiations turn into disputes. Before paying, confirm the seller’s identity, ownership chain, and authority to sell—especially where there are co-owners or inherited property claims.

Ask for clear copies of title documents, previous sale deed, mutation records, tax receipts, and society or builder NOCs (where applicable). For apartments, check occupancy status, maintenance dues, and any pending litigation notices.

If you are buying from a builder, review allotment letter, builder-buyer agreement, RERA details, payment schedule, and possession timelines. Many disputes originate from vague clauses and missing annexures.

Avoid informal cash commitments or verbal assurances. Put every promise into writing: fittings, parking, possession date, penalty, and refund terms. A short written addendum can prevent months of dispute.
Due diligence is not about distrust—it is about clarity. A careful checklist protects your money and your timeline.
